coredata.dc:title: Optimizing molybdenum oxide based RRAM with vacuum rapid thermal annealing and carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: The performance of resistive random access memory (RRAM) is significantly influenced by the dielectric layer and interface. In this study, the vacuum rapid thermal annealing (VacRTA) process and the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were employed to enhance the device performance. The VacRTA process induced extra oxygen vacancies in the MoOx film, reducing the energy required for the formation of Ag conductive filaments (CFs). In addition, CQDs were spin-coated on the bottom electrode (BE) interface to effectively fix the position of Ag CFs formation and rupture. The improved device exhibits remarkable performance enhancements compared to the pristine device, including a centralized distribution of resistance states and operating voltages, extended endurance cycles (1 × 107), enhanced cell-to-cell coherence, and favorable neural synaptic properties. These results demonstrate the potential of CQDs incorporated MoOx based RRAM for next generation non-volatile memory and neural synapse simulation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The performance of resistive random access memory (RRAM) is significantly influenced by the dielectric layer and interface. In this study, the vacuum rapid thermal annealing (VacRTA) process and the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were employed to enhance the device performance. The VacRTA process induced extra oxygen vacancies in the MoOx film, reducing the energy required for the formation of Ag conductive filaments (CFs). In addition, CQDs were spin-coated on the bottom electrode (BE) interface to effectively fix the position of Ag CFs formation and rupture. The improved device exhibits remarkable performance enhancements compared to the pristine device, including a centralized distribution of resistance states and operating voltages, extended endurance cycles (1 × 107), enhanced cell-to-cell coherence, and favorable neural synaptic properties. These results demonstrate the potential of CQDs incorporated MoOx based RRAM for next generation non-volatile memory and neural synapse simulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays traditional memory is struggling to meet the escalating demand for significant improvements in read/write speed and storage capacity. Therefore, researchers have endeavored to develop novel memory technologies [1], among which RRAM has gained widespread attention due to its high read/write speed, elevated storage density and non-volatility, positioning it as a strong candidate for the next generation non-volatile memory [2]. Within the RRAM, the electrochemical metallization (ECM) type of RRAM has been favored by researchers due to its mature resistive switching mechanism and simple structure. However, conventional ECM-type RRAM faces challenges such as poor device stability, limited endurance cycles, and inconsistent cell-to-cell performance [3]. This is because ECM-type RRAM relies on metal ion migration and redox reactions to achieve resistive switching [4]. The migration of metal ions exhibits high randomness, leading to variability in the formation and rupture of metal CFs [5]. Therefore, controlling the migration of metal ions to fix the position of metal CFs formation and rupture becomes crucial for improving the performance of ECM-type RRAM.

Molybdenum oxide, as a well-known semiconductor material, is widely used in various fields such as gas sensors, solar cells, and electrochromic devices due to its wide bandgap and favorable optoelectronic properties [6]. However, its application as a resistive switching layer for RRAM has received limited attention. Most existing research on molybdenum oxide based RRAM primarily focuses on the valence change mechanism (VCM) type [7,8], with few studies exploring the ECM-type. Notably, Dongsoo Lee et al. successfully fabricated the first ECM-type RRAM with a Cu/Cu-doped MoOx/Pt structure [9], achieving remarkable endurance with 1 × 106 pulse cycles and a retention time of 1 × 105 s at 85 °C. Additionally, they performed conducting atomic force microscopy (CAFM) tests to investigate the formation and rupture of CFs. These findings convincingly demonstrate the feasibility of utilizing MoOx as a material for ECM-type RRAM. Subsequently, M. Kudo et al. performed in-situ transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and directly observed Cu CFs in Cu/MoOx/TiN structures for the first time [10]. This observation further confirms the effective migration of metal ions and the formation of CFs within MoOx film. Despite the significant potential of MoOx in ECM-type RRAM, there remains ample room for improvement in the resistive switching performance.

In this study, extra oxygen vacancies were introduced into MoOx film by VacRTA process. This method causes a portion of the lattice oxygen within the MoOx film to detach from its original lattice position, spill out onto the film surface, and subsequently generate extra oxygen vacancies within the film. These extra oxygen vacancies play a crucial role in significantly reducing the energy required for the drift of Ag+ ions within the film [11]. Consequently, the facilitation of Ag CFs formation becomes easier, thereby considerably enhancing the device stability. Building upon this foundation, CQDs were added to further enhance device performance. The incorporation of CQDs serves to intensify the electric field at the BE interface [12], fixing the position of metal CFs formation and rupture. In the past, researchers have reduced the randomness by inserting metal nanoparticles [13], shaping electrode [14] and other methods of generating nanoscale protrusions to induce the regular formation and rupture of metal CFs [15]. The method of inserting metal nanoparticles requires additional complex processes such as electron beam evaporation and annealing. It is challenging to achieve the formation of smaller and uniformly distributed metal nanoparticles using this approach. Alternatively, the method of shaping electrode also involves additional complex processes, including photolithography and etching. In contrast, the incorporation of CQDs involved a straightforward spin-coating process, offering more environmentally friendly as well as biocompatible [16] option than other heavy metal QDs such as PbS QDs [17] and CdS QDs [18]. With the extra oxygen vacancies provided by VacRTA and the electric field concentration effect of CQDs, the improved device effectively controls the formation and rupture position of Ag CFs, which significantly improves the resistive switching performance and stability. Furthermore, the study delves into the device performance in synapse simulation.

In order to analyze the impact of VacRTA process and CQDs on device performance, this study prepared CQDs device (Ag/VacRTA MoOx/CQDs/Pt), VacRTA device (Ag/VacRTA MoOx/Pt), and pristine device (Ag/MoOx/Pt) for comparison. The pristine device underwent the following fabrication steps: Initially, a Ti film was deposited on an N-type silicon substrate with 111-crystal orientation using DC magnetron sputtering at a power of 30 W. This Ti film served as an adhesion layer for the Pt electrode and the silicon substrate. Subsequently, the Pt film was grown as the BE via RF magnetron sputtering at 80 W. The layer of MoOx film was then deposited using RF magnetron sputtering at 50 W, employing a mixed gas atmosphere of Ar and O2 (40:10) at 350 °C. Finally, an Ag film was deposited as the top electrode (TE) through electron beam evaporation at 5 W, using a mask with a diameter of 300 μm. For the VacRTA device, on the basis of the pristine device fabrication process, after the MoOx film grown by magnetron sputtering, the MoOx film was processed by RTA in a high vacuum environment (3.5 × 10−6 Torr), and then the Ag TE with a diameter of 300 μm mask was prepared by electron beam evaporation process, just as in the previous process. As for the CQDs device, after preparing the Pt film, 30 μL of CQDs solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL was spin-coated onto the Pt film five times at 3000 rpm. After that, the MoOx film was grown by magnetron sputtering under the same conditions as before, and then the MoOx film was treated under the same VacRTA process as before. Finally, a 300 μm diameter Ag film was prepared as the TE using the same electron beam evaporation process and mask. Moreover, the CQD device was also subjected to TEM characterization. This involved FIB cutting to obtain a cross-section sample for examination. As shown in Fig. 1 , the TEM image clearly shows all interfaces within the device. The Ag layer is 180 nm, the MoOx layer is 65 nm which shows a uniform amorphous state and the Pt layer is 200 nm.

The crystal structure of the MoOx film was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD: Rigaku SmartLab 9 KW). The elemental composition and valence states were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS: Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi). The samples have not undergone sputter etching. During the XPS analyses period, the excitation source used characteristic Kα lines generated from Al anodes. The electron emission angle was 90°. The analyzed area was a circular region with a diameter of 500 μm. Charge neutralizer was employed, where the base pressure during analyses was about 3.5 × 10−7 mTorr. Since the method of aligning the spectra to the C 1s peak of adventitious carbon is not reliable [19–21], this study uses the work function method for calibrating XPS results to ensure the reliability [22]. According to the previous research, annealing at 400 °C has no significant effect on the work function of MoOx [23]. Therefore, the work function (ΦSA) of both the as-deposited MoOx and the VacRTA MoOx samples is 5.3 eV [24]. And the calibrated C 1s binding energy (E_B) for both samples is 284.28 eV, as determined by the work function method. And the device cross-section was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM: FEI Talos F200X). In addition, after VacRTA process, the Pt film and CQDs/Pt film's surface topography and root mean square (RMS) roughness were characterized using atomic force microscopy (AFM: BRUKER Dimension Icon). Finally, the resistive switching performance of the three devices were tested using a Keysight B1500A semiconductor parameter analyzer.

As shown in Fig. 2 , different MoOx film samples were prepared for XRD characterization. The results indicate that the MoOx film whether or not treated by VacRTA process, also remains in the amorphous state, with the significant diffraction peak in the figure attributed to Pt film. For further investigation, XPS analysis was performed to determine the elemental composition and valence states in both as-deposited MoOx and VacRTA MoOx film. Fig. 3 (a) and (e) depict the XPS survey spectrum of both samples, revealing all elements present at the sample surface. The fitting peaks of C 1s are shown in Fig. 3 (b) and (f), with the peak position at 284.28 eV corresponding to C–C bonds [25], while the peak position at 285.76 eV and 285.63 eV correspond to C–O bonds [26]. The presence of C–C and C–O bonds in these samples can be attributed to the adsorption of adventitious carbon from air, which occurred due to the absence of surface sputter etching. As shown in Fig. 3 (c) and (g), the binding energy of the Mo6+ (3/2 orbital: 235.45 eV and 5/2 orbital: 232.31 eV) remains unchanged after VacRTA process [27]. However, the binding energy of the 3/2 orbitals of the Mo5+ slightly decrease from 235.08 eV to 235.06 eV, while the binding energy of the 5/2 orbitals slightly increase from 231.78 eV to 231.81 eV [28]. Thus, it can be inferred that VacRTA process does not significantly impact the binding energy position of Mo elements in MoOx film. Nevertheless, there is a change in the peak area ratio of Mo elements, with the peak area ratio of Mo5+ increasing from 15.24 % before VacRTA process to 16.41 %. This suggests that the MoOx film treated by VacRTA process is more inclined towards Mo5+ state, corresponding to the extra oxygen vacancies created by the lattice oxygen overflow after VacRTA process. Next, as depicted in Fig. 3 (d) and (h), the peaks of O 1s before and after VacRTA process of MoOx films were fitted. In this fitting, the peak position at 532.04 eV and 531.95 eV can be attributed to CO specie according to the self-consistent model [29], OV (530.47 eV, 530.59 eV) represents oxygen vacancies, and OL (530 eV) represents lattice oxygen [30]. Notably, the peak area ratio of oxygen vacancies increased from 32.76 % to 38.52 % after VacRTA process, accompanied by a slight increase in the binding energy of the oxygen vacancies. These findings underscore that VacRTA process induced MoOx film to generate extra oxygen vacancies, transforming part of the Mo6+ to Mo5+.

These devices were electrically tested by applying sweep voltage to the TE and ground to the BE with 3 mA compliance current. Fig. 4 illustrate the I–V curves obtained from 50 resistive switching cycles, where red, yellow, and blue representing the typical I–V curves of each device, and gray indicating the remaining 49 cycles of I–V curves. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the pristine device shows instability during the resistive switching cycles, which significantly affects its application in the memory array. Compare to the pristine device, in Fig. 4 (b) the storage window of the VacRTA device has narrowed, but it shows relatively better cycle to cycle stability. For further comparison, the operating voltage distribution is extracted from the IV curves, as depicted in Fig. 5 (a). The set voltage distribution of the pristine device is more dispersed, reaching a coefficient of variation (σ/μ) of 16.2 %. And the set voltage distribution of the VacRTA device is more concentrated than the pristine device but remains relatively dispersed, with a σ/μ of 12.6 %. Regarding the reset voltage, the pristine device has a more dispersed reset voltage distribution, with a σ/μ of 17.8 %. Meanwhile, the reset voltage distribution of the VacRTA device shows a larger degree of dispersion, with a σ/μ of 23.1 %. Simultaneously, the resistance distribution is shown in Fig. 5 (b), pristine device has significant fluctuations in HRS with a σ/μ as high as 293 %. It can be influenced by the random rupture of CFs during the reset process. However, the LRS of the pristine device exhibits smaller fluctuations, with a σ/μ of 10.4 %. It may due to the formation of only a single CFs during the set process. For the VacRTA device, the fluctuation of the resistance distribution of HRS is relatively small with a σ/μ of 19.9 %, while the fluctuation of LRS is larger with a σ/μ of 26 %. This result indicates that the VacRTA device has a relatively stable resistance distribution at HRS but exhibits large fluctuations at LRS. It can be attributed to that underwent the VacRTA process, the extra oxygen vacancies will increase the conductivity of the MoOx film causes the HRS decrease. Then, it maybe also facilitates much more Ag + ions to drift through the MoOx film makes the larger fluctuation of LRS and reset voltage. As shown in Fig. 5 (c), it is evident that all devices maintain a stable retention of 1 × 104 s under 85 °C. However, notable fluctuations are observed in both HRS and LRS for the pristine device, while the VacRTA device also exhibits some HRS fluctuations, although its LRS remains relatively stable. Endurance performance is one of the important tests for evaluating device performance. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (d), the pristine device exhibits dramatic HRS fluctuations, which align with the I–V curves. It is capable of achieving only 2400 endurance cycles. In contrast, the VacRTA device, building upon a relatively lower level of HRS fluctuations, extends endurance cycles to 3500, although some fluctuations persist in its LRS. The above results can be attributed to the fact that ECM-type devices primarily rely on metal ion migration to form and rupture metal CFs during the resistive switching process. The pristine device lacks extra oxygen vacancies and can forms thin Ag CFs, leading to random rupture of Ag CFs and sharp fluctuations in HRS during the reset process. For the VacRTA device, the extra oxygen vacancies generated by VacRTA process can make the Ag CFs thicker, effectively reducing the randomness of Ag CFs rupture during reset process. Nevertheless, the existence of extra oxygen vacancies also increases the conductivity of the film, resulting in decreased HRS. While Ag CFs are easier to form, they may tend to form multi-filament structures, causing fluctuations in LRS.

The conduction mechanisms of these devices were further investigated to determine their resistive switching mechanism, and Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the redrawn double logarithmic plot of the I–V curves under positive voltage sweep. Firstly, under low voltage conditions, the I–V curves for both devices exhibit a slope of approximately 1, obeying to Ohm's law. This is attributed to the low electric field generated by the low voltage, causing the injected electrons to be captured by the traps in the MoOx film, resulting in a limited number of free electrons and consequently a low current. As the voltage increases, the slope becomes approximately 2, in accordance with Child's law. As the voltage is further increased, the set process occurs, switching the resistance from HRS to LRS and causing a dramatic increase in current. This is due to the increase in the electric field formed as the voltage rises, causing the injected electrons to gradually fill the traps in MoOx film. Once the traps are filled, the subsequent injected electrons act as free electrons due to the lack of trap capture. At this point, the current increases sharply, and the change in slope conforms to the space charge limited current conduction (SCLC) mechanism [31]. During the negative sweep voltage in LRS, the slope is approximately 1, indicating free electron conduction without traps, consistent with the ohmic conduction mechanism. Therefore, the slope aligns with the SCLC mechanism in HRS, explaining that the traps in the MoOx film, possibly consisting of lattice oxygen or Ag+ ions [32], trapping the injected electrons. This process gradually forms oxygen vacancies and Ag CFs. In LRS, consistent with the ohmic conduction mechanism, this implies the presence of a fully formed CFs. By explaining these two conduction mechanisms, it can be concluded that the resistive switching behavior of both devices is controlled by the CFs.

To identify the type of CFs, temperature dependent I–V tests were performed in the LRS and HRS of these devices over a range of 300 K–350 K and a voltage range of 0–0.2 V. Fig. 7 (a) and (c) depict the LRS resistance ratio of both devices at different temperatures (0.2 V read voltage). The observed trend reveals an increase in the resistance ratio with increasing temperature, which is consistent with the temperature resistance property of metals [33]. Thus, the LRS is due to the presence of metal CFs, confirming that both devices belong to the ECM type. The fitting is then performed according to equation R(T) = R(T0) [1 + α(T - T0)], where R(T) represents the LRS at different temperatures (T), R(T0) is the LRS at the initial temperature (T0), and α is the temperature coefficient obtained from the fitting slope [34]. In Fig. 7 (a), the temperature coefficient for the pristine device is found to be 3.09 × 10−3 K−1, which is smaller than the reported temperature coefficient of single crystal defect-free Ag nanowires (approximately 4 × 10−3 K−1) [35]. This suggests that the Ag CFs in the pristine device are relatively fine. In Fig. 7 (c), the temperature coefficient for the VacRTA device is 3.55 × 10−3 K−1, indicating that the Ag CFs are slightly more robust than those in the pristine device due to the moderate introduction of oxygen vacancies during VacRTA process [36]. Next, the temperature dependent I–V test at HRS was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 7 (b) and (d), the activation energy curves were obtained by replotting the temperature dependent I–V in HRS by the form of Arrhenius and fitting the slopes [37]. In Fig. 7 (b), the activation energy for the pristine device is 0.114 eV, which represents the energy required to rupture Ag CFs during the reset process [38]. This small value indicates that the Ag CFs formed in the pristine device are relatively small, making them prone to rupture during the reset process, leading to significant randomness in HRS. In Fig. 7 (d), the activation energy of the VacRTA device is 0.178 eV, higher than that of the pristine device. It can be concluded that the VacRTA process generates the extra oxygen vacancies in the surface of the MoOx film. The Ag + ions can directly insert into these extra oxygen vacancies and reduce the energy required for direct drift in the MoOx film. Consequently, this facilitates the formation of more robust Ag CFs, which are more durable to rupture during the reset process and improve the stability of HRS.

Although the simple VacRTA process can relatively improve the device performance compared to the pristine device. But the improvements remain much limited and bring the new problem like the LRS becomes relatively unstable. Nanocomposite structures are employed in a multitude of applications, including photocatalytic degradation, absorption, and various devices [39–42]. In particular, the incorporation of nanocomposite structures to enhance the performance of RRAM devices represents a crucial approach. Consequently, a comprehensive analysis of the CQDs device is conducted to explore the enhancements in device performance attributable to the incorporation of CQDs. Firstly, to characterize the presence of CQDs, the as-deposited Pt, VacRTA Pt, and VacRTA CQDs/Pt samples were prepared for AFM test. Examining the height image, Fig. 8 (a) shows the relatively flat surface of the as-deposited Pt sample, exhibiting a root mean square (RMS) roughness of only 1.14 nm. In contrast, Fig. 8 (b) shows the surface of the VacRTA Pt sample, which has a slightly higher RMS roughness of 1.77 nm. In Fig. 8 (c), the surface of the VacRTA CQDs/Pt sample shows a mesh structure with nanoscale protrusions. And the RMS roughness reaches 6.64 nm, indicating a significant increase compared to the previous two samples. Considering the phase image, the surface of the as-deposited Pt sample exhibits poor phase uniformity (Fig. 8 (d)). Conversely, the phase uniformity of the VacRTA Pt sample (Fig. 8 (e)) is significantly improved, suggesting that the VacRTA process makes the Pt surface more uniform and slightly improves the RMS roughness. Regarding the surface of the VacRTA CQDs/Pt sample, a significant phase shift is observed (Fig. 8 (f)), indicating that these nanoscale protrusions are formed by the spin-coating of CQDs rather than by the VacRTA process treated substrate.

Then, as shown in the inset of Fig. 4, the forming voltage of the three devices gradually decreases, especially for the CQDs device, where the forming voltage is 0.8 V, significantly lower than that of the pristine device and the VacRTA device. This reduction can be attributed to the fact that the incorporation of CQDs corresponds to nanoscale protrusions, causing the uniform electric field lines generated when the sweep voltage was applied to bend around the CQDs and become concentrated. As a result, Ag+ ions are more likely to form Ag CFs around the CQDs, requiring lower forming voltage to complete the electroforming process [43]. And as shown in Fig. 4 (c) the CQDs device also has the best I–V cycle to cycle consistency over the other devices. For the set voltage, the CQDs device displays a relatively more concentrated distribution with a standard deviation coefficient (σ/μ) of 10.7 %, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). Regarding the reset voltage, the CQDs device exhibits a more centralized feature with a σ/μ of 9.2 %. Overall, compared with the pristine and VacRTA device, the CQDs device demonstrates better concentration in the operating voltage distribution. Simultaneously, the CQDs device also exhibits the most concentrated resistance distribution (0.1 V read voltage), as shown in Fig. 5 (b). In terms of HRS, its σ/μ is 10.4 %, while in terms of LRS, the σ/μ is 10.6 %, indicating the relative stability of the resistance distribution. For the retention test, the CQDs device remains relatively stable in both LRS and HRS, indicating the excellent thermal retention stability, as shown in Fig. 5 (c). Finally, endurance resistance fluctuations in the CQDs device are significantly suppressed, and the DC endurance number is extended to 1 × 104 cycles, as shown in Fig. 5 (d). Following the DC endurance test, the pulse endurance test reveals that the CQDs device can achieve 1 × 107 cycles without significant storage window decay. The cell-to-cell consistency within the memory array serves as a crucial indicator of its stability and reliability. 16 cells were extracted from each of the three devices following a 4 × 4 matrix configuration, and the cell-to-cell consistency distribution is depicted in Fig. 9 (a) and (b). The analysis shows that the CQDs device has the most centralized consistency in both the operating voltage distribution and the resistance distribution. This study also compared CQDs device with previously reported MoOx-based devices, as shown in Table 1 . Obviously, the CQDs device demonstrates large ON-OFF ratio, low operating voltage, ultra-long endurance cycles, and synaptic properties.

The I–V curve fitting of the CQDs device was performed. Fig. 6 (c) shows that the CQDs device also follows the SCLC mechanism. Then, the temperature dependent I–V test in LRS was performed. And the abstracted LRS resistance ratio is shown in Fig. 7 (e), the temperature coefficient of the CQDs device is 3.94 × 10−3 K−1, which is very close to that of single crystal defect-free Ag nanowires and being the highest among the three devices. This suggests that the Ag CFs in the CQDs device have fewer defects and are more robust. As shown in Fig. 7 (f), the activation energy curve was obtained by replotting the temperature dependent I–V in HRS by the form of Arrhenius and fitting the slopes. It shows the activation energy of HRS for the CQDs device is 0.295 eV, which is the highest among the three devices. It can be concluded that the incorporation of CQDs effectively enhances the durability of Ag CFs and inhibits the random rupture of Ag CFs during the reset process, thereby extending the endurance cycles.

Based on the above results, the model diagram depicted in Fig. 10 can be summarized to provide a more detailed explanation of the resistive switching mechanism. Before that, the forming process of the devices are introduced. Initially, the devices are in their initial state with no CFs in the MoOx film. When a positive voltage is applied, the TE is connected to the anode of the power supply, undergoes an oxidation reaction Ag – e−→Ag+. Then, the generated Ag+ ions drift under the positive electric field. When the Ag+ ions reach the BE, a reduction reaction: Ag+ + e−→Ag takes place because the BE is connected to the cathode of the power supply. The oxidation-reduction reaction continues, with subsequently arriving Ag+ ions being reduced near the Ag atoms formed in the previous step, rather than at the BE. As a result, continuous Ag CFs gradually form from the BE toward the TE, and the reaction stops when these CFs connect the BE and TE [44]. This process is called electroforming process. And the voltage required for this process is known as the forming voltage. After electroforming process, for the ECM-type RRAM, the set and reset processes are not the complete formation and rupture of the entire CFs. In fact, these processes are only controlled by the formation and rupture of the narrowest region of the CFs [45]. For the pristine device, the electric field generated during the positive voltage in the set process is uniformly distributed in the MoOx film. As a result, Ag+ ions randomly land on the Pt BE, accomplishing the set process from HRS to LRS. The small temperature coefficient (3.09 × 10−3 K−1) of the pristine device indicates that the formed Ag CFs have many defects and are relatively fine, as shown in Fig. 10 (c). In the reset process of the pristine device, a negative voltage is applied, causing a single Ag CFs at a random location near the Pt BE to rupture. And the activation energy of the HRS in the pristine device is 0.114 eV, indicating that the Ag CFs can easily rupture and switch to HRS, resulting in a large fluctuation, as depicted in Fig. 10 (e). For the VacRTA device, the VacRTA process introduces extra oxygen vacancies in the MoOx film. The Ag + ions can directly insert into these oxygen vacancies, thereby reducing the energy required for direct drift in the MoOx film. The LRS temperature coefficient of the VacRTA device has increased to 3.55 × 10−3 K−1 compared to the pristine device. This is due to more Ag + ions drifting under the electric field in the set process, forming multiple Ag CFs as shown in Fig. 10 (a). However, these CFs are still randomly formed, increasing the fluctuation of the LRS compared to the pristine device. In the reset process, the higher activation energy (0.178 eV) indicates that the multiple Ag CFs are less prone to rupture, thus reducing the randomness, as shown in Fig. 10 (b). Finally, for the CQDs device, it also underwent the VacRTA process to facilitate the formation of Ag CFs, similar to the VacRTA device. However, the extra oxygen vacancies will make the LRS of the device unstable due to the random formation of multiple Ag CFs, without fundamentally addressing the issue of Ag+ ions randomly landing on the Pt BE during resistive switching. Therefore, CQDs were added to overcome this issue. In the set process, the CQDs are located at the interface between the Pt BE and the MoOx film, causing the electric field generated by applying a positive voltage to be uneven. The partial electric field near the Pt BE bends and concentrates around the CQDs, resulting in a higher electric field intensity compared to other areas. This concentration facilitates the attraction of Ag+ ions to the CQDs during the drift process toward the Pt BE, leading to the capture of electrons and reduction to Ag atoms. In other words, introducing CQDs fixes the position of Ag+ ions drifting toward the Pt BE relatively at the CQDs, reducing the randomness of the LRS and significantly extending its endurance. According to the LRS temperature coefficient of the CQDs device of 3.94 × 10−3 K−1, it is closest to the temperature coefficient of single crystal defect-free Ag nanowires. Therefore, after the set process, a single, more concentrated Ag CFs is formed near the Pt BE, as shown in Fig. 10 (d). As for the reset process, under the negative electric field, the Ag atoms around the CQDs also lose electrons and turn into Ag+ ions. This locally ruptures the Ag CFs around the CQDs and switches to HRS, as shown in Fig. 10 (f). At the same time, the HRS activation energy of the CQDs device is 0.295 eV, the highest among the three devices. This significantly suppresses the randomness of the HRS of the CQDs device.

The simulation of neural synapses, as the fundamental unit that replicates the learning and memory functions in an organism's brain [46,47], which is important in neural network research. Expanding the utility of the CQDs device to simulate neural synapses lays the foundation for future integrated applications in neural network computing and storage. The analog I–V with cycle-to-cycle variability is shown in Fig. 11 (a), the current of CQDs device gradually increases with the continuous application of DC positive sweep voltage (0.32 V), while the opposite is observed with the continuous application of DC negative voltage (−0.32 V), where the device current gradually decreases. Subsequently, the current-voltage response concerning time is depicted in Fig. 11 (b). Applying six consecutive positive spike voltages (0.32 V, 10 s) and six consecutive negative spike voltages (−0.32 V, 10 s) also causes the device current to increase and decrease with the number of pulse voltages. These results indicate that CQDs devices can be used as a synapse to simulate neural activity [48]. Fig. 12 illustrates the long-term potentiation (LTP) and long-term depression (LTD) normalized conductance distribution of the three devices. In this test, 100 consecutive positive pulse voltages (0.37 V, 100 ms) were applied, followed by a 0.1 V pulse read voltage. The current exhibits a continuous increase with the growing number of pulse voltages, indicating a progressively larger conductance and representing the LTP behavior. Then, 100 consecutive negative pulse voltages (−0.16 V, 100 ms) were applied, resulting in a continuous decrease in current and read-out conductance value as the number of pulse voltages increases, indicating LTD behavior. Although all three devices exhibit LTP and LTD behaviors, but both the pristine device and the VacRTA device have relatively large nonlinear factors (αp/d) and more dispersed conductance values. In contrast, the CQDs device demonstrates the most concentrated distribution of conductance values and the smallest nonlinear factors. It suggesting that the CQDs device exhibits favorable linear continuous conductance modulation behavior. The above results can be attributed to the CQDs device's fixed formation/rupture position of Ag CFs, allowing for a more linear and continuous variation of conductance values under the consecutive pulse voltages. Furthermore, an application of artificial neural network (ANN) is to achieve the recognition of handwritten digit through training. Then a fully connected network (FCN) structure is employed, comprising 400 input neurons, 100 hidden neurons, and 10 output neurons, as illustrated in Fig. 13 (a) [49]. This structure was trained using 60,000 MNIST test images. The corresponding circuit structure is shown in Fig. 13 (b) [50]. And Fig. 13 (c) presents the inference accuracy results for each device, while the synaptic parameters were obtained by Fig. 12 Through 125 rounds of training, the CQDs device achieved an inference accuracy of approximately 91 %, attributed to its excellent linear LTP and LTD behaviors, demonstrating the potential of CQDs device in handwritten digit recognition. In contrast, the VacRTA device and the pristine device exhibited lower inference accuracies of approximately 86 % and 79 %, respectively.

Fig. 14 illustrates the transition from STP/D to LTP/D in the CQDs device by maintaining a constant number of pulse voltages and varying the pulse voltage amplitude. In Fig. 14 (a)–(c), a positive pulse voltage (0.35 V, 5 ms) was applied, causing an abrupt increase in device current, which is maintained for approximately 4.5 ms before returning to the initial current state, representing the STP behavior. Subsequently, the pulse voltage was increased to 0.36 V, while keeping the pulse width constant at 5 ms. The maintain time of the device current further increases, but it still decreases to the initial current state, indicating an intermediate transition behavior. Finally, when the pulse voltage was increased to 0.37 V with a constant pulse width of 5 ms, the maintain time of the device current reaches its maximum, indicating the LTP behavior. Simultaneously, in Fig. 14 (d)–(f), a negative pulse voltage (−0.14 V, 5 ms) was applied to the device, resulting in a sudden reduction in device current, which is maintained for about 2.5 ms before returning to the initial current state and this behavior is considered as STD. With a larger negative pulse voltage (−0.15 V, 5 ms), the device current is maintained longer before returning to the initial state, representing an intermediate transition behavior. When the negative pulse voltage was further increased to −0.16 V, the maintain time of the device current reaches its maximum, indicating the LTD behavior. These findings demonstrate the capability of the CQDs device to undergo the transition from STP/D to LTP/D by adjusting the pulse voltage amplitude, providing a foundation for the realization of more complex neural network systems.

In ECM-type RRAM, the set and reset processes involve the partial formation and rupture of metal CFs. The principle underlying the transition from STP to LTP in CQDs device can be similar to the set process. During the small positive pulse voltage was applied, a fraction of the Ag+ ions are influenced by the electric field to form fine Ag CFs, resulting in a transient increase in device current. When the positive pulse voltage was removed, these small Ag CFs cannot be sustained and promptly rupture, returning the device current to its initial state and placing the device in STP. As the positive pulse voltage was increased, more Ag+ ions contribute to the formation of thicker Ag CFs. When the applied positive pulse voltage reaches a sufficient level, the formed Ag CFs become robust enough to endure for an extended period without rupture, representing LTP behavior. Similarly, the transition from STD to LTD in the CQDs device can be analogized to the reset process. As the applied negative pulse voltage increases, an increasing number of Ag CFs rupture into Ag+ ions. When the applied negative pulse becomes substantial enough to induce more extensive rupture of Ag CFs, the device can be maintained for a longer period of time without returning to the initial current state, thus representing the LTD behavior.

Paired Pulse Facilitation (PPF) and Paired Pulse Depression (PPD) are part of the short-term synaptic plasticity that influences neuronal activity. By applying two identical positive pulse voltages (0.39 V, 50 ms) with different pulse intervals (25 ms, 50 ms, 100 ms, 200 ms, 400 ms, 800 ms) and then calculating the difference between the currents induced by the second and the first pulse (I2 - I1), followed by exponential fitting, PPF fitting curves are obtained as Fig. 15 (a). Examination of the PPF fitting curve reveals that with shorter pulse intervals (25 ms, 50 ms, 100 ms), the disparity in paired pulse current is more significant, indicating a more noticeable increase in device current at these intervals. As the pulse interval increases (200 ms, 400 ms, 800 ms), the difference in paired pulse current decreases, indicating an even negligible current increase, consistent with the behavior of PPF. Similarly, the PPD fitting curve depicted in Fig. 15 (b) also consistent with the behavior of PPD, where the inhibition effect on device current becomes less apparent with increasing pulse intervals.

Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity (STDP) is a form of synaptic plasticity that is based on temporal differences in pulse activity between neurons, also known as Hebbian learning method [51]. The time interval between pre and post stimulation stands out as the most important factor influencing the STDP mechanism. In this test, the CQDs device underwent the two symmetric pulse voltages (±0.41 V, 50 ms). The STDP mechanism is implemented by modulating the time intervals between these two pulse voltages (Δt = 60 ms, 80 ms, 100 ms, 120 ms, 140 ms, 160 ms). The Δω/ω in the figure represents the rate of change of synaptic weights, analogous to the rate of change of the device current. Here, ω denotes the initial synaptic weight corresponding to the initial device current, while Δω represents the difference between the final and initial synaptic weights, corresponding to the difference between the final and initial device current. The fitting results are depicted in Fig. 16 , where the red line represents the fitting results of LTP and the gray line represents the fitting results of LTD. Notably, the absolute values of Δω/ω decrease as Δt increases, indicating the STDP behavior of the CQDs device.

This study examines the impact of CQDs and VacRTA on MoOx based ECM-type RRAM is investigated. The CQDs device demonstrates prolonged endurance cycles (1 × 107), concentrated set/reset voltage distribution, significantly reduced forming voltage (∼0.8 V), improved stability in both the HRS and LRS distribution. The incorporation of CQDs effectively controls the formation and rupture position of Ag CFs. And it is achieved by inducing a concentrated and curved electric field. This control significantly reduces the randomness during resistive switching, ultimately improving the resistive performance and stability of the device. Furthermore, through tests on analog I–V with cycle-to-cycle variability, current-voltage response concerning time, continuous conductance modulation, inference accuracy test in handwritten digit recognition, long and short-term plasticity transition, PPF/PPD and STDP to simulate neural synapses show that the CQDs device exhibits favorable neural synaptic properties. Such improvements and properties can be used in the future for non-volatile memory and artificial neural network applications.
